description: Having a structure in which the premises entail the conclusion. Validity is a fact about the argument's shape, and a valid argument can still rest on a false premise, since truth and structure are two separate questions.
sound_symbolism: The clean edge of 'ki' begins with a structural test. 'The' introduces deliberate examination, and 'la' opens the whole form so its connections can be seen. The word is firm without sounding final or aggressive.
grammatical_notes: An adjective placed before a noun or used predicatively: 'kithela themore' (a valid argument); 'ha themore kithela nai.' (This argument is valid.) The quality-noun rule supplies validity. Distinct from 'shewo' (true), 'telua' (accurate), and 'norethi' (confident).

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Validity separates structural relation from attachment to a preferred conclusion, allowing form and factual premises to be examined independently.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Calling an argument valid need not endorse its premises, while calling it invalid need not condemn its speaker.

tags.cognition: the quality of an argument whose premises entail its conclusion
tags.wisdom: a precise assessment kept distinct from truth and persuasion
